---
title: "The operator algebra: sparse storage and semirings"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
Almost every operator in this unit is the same move: **gather a few neighbours and
reduce them**. A vertex talks to its 1-ring; a voxel talks to its window. Two ideas
carry the whole substrate.

- **ELL** is how we *store* the neighbour lists: two dense `(n, k_max)` arrays
  (`values` + `indices`), short rows padded with a no-op so every row is the same
  width — a fixed rectangular loop that XLA loves.
- A **semiring** is how we *choose the arithmetic*: a pair $(\oplus, \otimes)$ of a
  reduce and a combine. The same gather becomes **diffusion** under REAL
  $(+,\times)$, **dilation** under max-plus $(\max,+)$, and a **distance transform**
  under min-plus $(\min,+)$.

One gather, many algebras. `apply_operator(op, x, semiring=…)` is the seam that
hides whether `op` is a flat `ELL` (the icosphere) or a bucketed `SectionedELL`
(a real cortex), so the same algorithm code runs on both.
:::

This page is the **substrate**. The cotangent Laplacian
([Laplace–Beltrami](laplace-beltrami.qmd)), the surface spectrum
([spectral](spectral.qmd)), curvature ([curvature](curvature.qmd)), and the
volumetric morphology and distance transforms ([volumes](volumes.qmd)) are all,
underneath, the same two arrays and the same gather-and-reduce — only the algebra
and the neighbour map change. Learn the machine here and you will recognise it on
every later page. It assumes the [foundations](foundations.qmd) page: the
discretise-then-converge posture, and the **DIFF-JAX / HOST-CTOR / HOST-QA**
execution classes (and the rule *never call a HOST op inside a jitted loop*) that we
tag throughout.

## A mesh or a grid is a sparse neighbour map {#sec-neighbours}

Here is the motivating question, the one we return to. You have a cortical surface —
a triangle mesh with a few hundred thousand vertices — and a scalar painted on it
(a thickness map, a task contrast, a curvature estimate). You want to **smooth it
along the surface**: replace each vertex's value with a weighted average of its
neighbours. That is one operation. But the *same* mesh, with the *same* neighbour
structure, also has to support: growing a labelled region by one ring (a
morphological dilation), finding the graph distance from a seed region (a
shortest-path / distance transform), and pooling features to a coarser mesh. Four
different geometric jobs, one neighbour map.

What does "neighbour map" mean as linear algebra? An operator that mixes each
vertex with its neighbours is a **matrix** $A$, where $A_{ij} \ne 0$ exactly when
$j$ is a neighbour of $i$, and the matvec $(Ax)_i = \sum_j A_{ij} x_j$ does the
mixing. The obvious thing is to store $A$ as a dense $n \times n$ matrix and call
`jnp.matmul`. That fails immediately: a cortical mesh has $n \approx 3\times 10^5$
vertices, so a dense $A$ is $\sim 9\times 10^{10}$ entries — hundreds of gigabytes —
and almost all of them are zero, because each vertex has only **five or six**
neighbours. The cure is to store only the non-zeros. That is what *sparse* means,
and the rest of this section is about choosing the right sparse layout for the
shapes geometry actually produces.

The crucial fact about geometric adjacency is that it is **sparse but
structured**: not a random scatter of non-zeros, but *roughly the same small number
per row*. A well-meshed surface has valence clustered tightly around 6; a
3×3×3 voxel window has exactly 27 taps. This near-uniform row length is the property
the storage format will exploit.

## ELL storage {#sec-ell}

Start from the simplest sparse idea a reader would invent: for each row $i$, keep a
**list of its neighbours' column indices** and a matching **list of the weights**.
Row 4 of our hub example might be `indices = [3, 5, 6, 7, 1, 2]`,
`values = [w, w, w, w, w, w]`. This is a *ragged* structure — different rows have
different lengths — and ragged arrays are exactly what XLA cannot compile to a
fast kernel: every shape must be known and rectangular at trace time.

The fix is one idea: **pad every row to a common width** $k_{\max} = \max_i
\deg(i)$, and store the result as two ordinary dense arrays. This is the **ELL**
format (the name is historical, from ELLPACK). In `nitrix` it is the
[`ELL`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell.py)
dataclass, with exactly the fields you would guess:

- `values: (m, k_max)` — the non-pad weights, one row per output row;
- `indices: (m, k_max)` — the column each weight multiplies;
- `n_cols: int` — the implicit $n$-dimension (the outer dim of the operand the ELL
  will contract with);
- `identity` — the scalar the pad positions of `values` were filled with.

A row with fewer than $k_{\max}$ neighbours has its trailing slots **padded**, and
the padding is the whole trick. Two things must be true of a pad slot for it to be a
*true no-op* under the reduction:

1. its **value** is the **semiring identity** — the element that the reduce leaves
   untouched ($0$ for ordinary sums, so a padded term contributes nothing); and
2. its **index** is an **in-range sentinel** — any valid row of the operand — so the
   `gather` that fetches it never reads out of bounds. The gathered value is
   multiplied by the identity and discarded, so *which* in-range row it points at
   does not matter, only that it is in range.

`ELL` records the identity it used so downstream kernels know the convention. The
construction helper
[`ell_pad`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell.py)`(values,
indices, *, k_max, n_cols, identity=0.0, pad_index=0)` does exactly this: it fills
`values[i, k_i:]` with `identity` and `indices[i, k_i:]` with `pad_index` (which it
checks lies in `[0, n_cols)`), returning the padded `ELL`. **(HOST-CTOR** — the
ragged input has a data-dependent length; you call it once to build the operator.)

### The matvec is a fixed rectangular loop

Why go to this trouble? Because the matvec is now a loop with **no ragged shapes**.
The output for row $i$ is

$$
C[i, j] \;=\; \bigoplus_{p=0}^{k_{\max}-1} \Big(\, \text{values}[i, p] \;\otimes\; B[\text{indices}[i, p],\, j] \,\Big),
$$ {#eq-operators-ellmatvec}

where $p$ runs over the $k_{\max}$ slots of the row, $B$ is the dense operand we are
contracting against (a per-vertex field, shape `(n_cols, ncol)`), and
$(\oplus, \otimes)$ is the algebra — for now read them as $(+, \times)$. Naming each
piece: $\text{indices}[i, p]$ says *which* neighbour, $\text{values}[i, p]$ says
*how much*, the gather $B[\text{indices}[i, p]]$ fetches the neighbour's value, the
combine $\otimes$ weights it, and the reduce $\oplus$ folds the $k_{\max}$ terms into
one. The cost is a fixed $O(n \cdot k_{\max})$ — one perfectly rectangular pass, no
dynamic shapes, ideal for XLA. This is the
[`reference_semiring_ell_matmul`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/_reference.py)
kernel, which walks the $k_{\max}$ axis with `lax.fori_loop` and the algebra's
combine/reduce; we meet its public wrapper in [the apply seam](#sec-apply).

### Why ELL, and not CSR or BCOO

A linear-algebra reader will know **CSR** (compressed sparse row: a flat values
array plus per-row pointers) and may know JAX's **BCOO** (batched coordinate
format). Both are more *general* than ELL — they store genuinely ragged patterns
with no padding waste. ELL deliberately trades that generality for a property they
lack: **a single static shape**. CSR's per-row pointer offsets make the inner loop
length data-dependent, which forces either a scan with dynamic bounds or a
segment-sum — both awkward under `jit` and hard to keep differentiable with a clean
custom gradient. ELL's two fixed `(n, k_max)` arrays are ordinary dense tensors,
nothing more; they flow through `jit` / `vmap` / `grad` with no special handling. The design bet, recorded
in
[`sparse-specialisations.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/sparse-specialisations.md)
and [`ell-on-triton.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/ell-on-triton.md),
is a **thin dense pair, no `jax.experimental.sparse` dependency anywhere** — because
geometric adjacency is *structured* sparse (near-uniform degree), the padding waste
is small and the compile-and-differentiate story is clean. `ELL` is registered as a
JAX **pytree** (children `values` / `indices`, static aux `n_cols` / `identity`), so
it is itself a first-class operand: you can `jit` a function that takes an `ELL`, and
`grad` through it returns an `ELL` cotangent with `values` populated and a zero
(`float0`) `indices` cotangent — consistent with treating indices as integer
topology you never differentiate.

### The padding cliff, and SectionedELL

The bet has a failure mode, and it is worth seeing clearly. ELL pads *every* row to
the **global** $k_{\max}$. That is nearly free when degrees are uniform
($k_{\max} \approx \text{median degree}$, as on the icosphere). It becomes
**pathological** when one row is much longer than the rest: a single high-valence
hub, or a distance-thresholded neighbourhood in an irregular point cloud where
$k_{\max}$ can be 10–100× the median. Then most of the `(n, k_max)` grid is
identity padding — memory and FLOPs spent on no-ops. This is the **padding cliff**.
@fig-operators-ell shows it on a tiny graph: a path of eight vertices with one hub,
where the flat ELL wastes more than half its cells.

```{python}
#| label: fig-operators-ell
#| fig-cap: "Left: a small irregular adjacency stored as a flat ELL. Every row is padded to the global k_max=6 set by the single hub vertex; real cells (blue) carry a neighbour index, padded cells (grey, 'id') carry the semiring identity and an in-range sentinel index. More than half the grid is wasted padding — the padding cliff. Right: the same adjacency as a SectionedELL — rows are bucketed by ceil(log2(degree)), each bucket gets its own small k_max, and the total stored cells drop sharply."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# --- A small irregular adjacency on 8 vertices (a line with one "hub") -------
# Neighbour lists of varying length: most rows are short, one vertex is a hub.
adj = {
    0: [1],
    1: [0, 2],
    2: [1, 3],
    3: [2, 4],
    4: [3, 5, 6, 7, 1, 2],   # the hub: high valence
    5: [4],
    6: [4],
    7: [4],
}
n = len(adj)
k_max = max(len(v) for v in adj.values())  # global padding width

# Build the flat-ELL indices/values grids; mark which cells are real vs pad.
ell_idx = np.full((n, k_max), -1, dtype=int)   # -1 marks pad here for drawing
real = np.zeros((n, k_max), dtype=bool)
for i in range(n):
    nb = adj[i]
    ell_idx[i, : len(nb)] = nb
    real[i, : len(nb)] = True

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4.8))

# ---- Left: flat ELL, padding cliff ----
A = ax[0]
for i in range(n):
    for p in range(k_max):
        is_real = real[i, p]
        color = "#9ecae1" if is_real else "#f0f0f0"
        A.add_patch(Rectangle((p, n - 1 - i), 1, 1, facecolor=color,
                              edgecolor="0.4", lw=0.8))
        txt = str(ell_idx[i, p]) if is_real else "id"
        A.text(p + 0.5, n - 1 - i + 0.5, txt, ha="center", va="center",
               fontsize=9, color=("k" if is_real else "0.6"))
A.set_xlim(0, k_max); A.set_ylim(0, n)
A.set_xticks(np.arange(k_max) + 0.5); A.set_xticklabels(range(k_max))
A.set_yticks(np.arange(n) + 0.5); A.set_yticklabels(range(n - 1, -1, -1))
A.set_xlabel("slot p  (0 .. k_max-1)"); A.set_ylabel("row i (vertex)")
n_real = real.sum(); n_pad = real.size - n_real
A.set_title(f"flat ELL: (n, k_max)=({n},{k_max})\n"
            f"{n_real} real cells, {n_pad} identity-padded "
            f"({100*n_pad/real.size:.0f}% wasted)")
A.set_aspect("equal")

# ---- Right: SectionedELL, rows bucketed by ceil(log2(degree)) ----
def bucket(d):
    return 0 if d <= 1 else (d - 1).bit_length()

buckets = {}
for i in range(n):
    b = bucket(len(adj[i]))
    buckets.setdefault(b, []).append(i)

B = ax[1]
y = 0
total_sectioned = 0
palette = ["#a1d99b", "#fdae6b", "#bcbddc", "#fa9fb5"]
for b in sorted(buckets):
    rows = buckets[b]
    bk = 1 if b == 0 else 2 ** b           # bucket-local k_max
    bk = max(bk, max(len(adj[i]) for i in rows))
    total_sectioned += len(rows) * bk
    for r, i in enumerate(rows):
        nb = adj[i]
        for p in range(bk):
            is_real = p < len(nb)
            color = palette[b % len(palette)] if is_real else "#f0f0f0"
            B.add_patch(Rectangle((p, y), 1, 1, facecolor=color,
                                  edgecolor="0.4", lw=0.8))
            txt = str(nb[p]) if is_real else "id"
            B.text(p + 0.5, y + 0.5, txt, ha="center", va="center",
                   fontsize=9, color=("k" if is_real else "0.6"))
        B.text(-0.4, y + 0.5, f"v{i}", ha="right", va="center", fontsize=8)
        y += 1
    B.text(-1.7, y - len(rows) / 2, f"bucket {b}\nk={bk}", ha="center",
           va="center", fontsize=8, color="0.3")
    y += 0.4

B.set_xlim(-2.2, k_max); B.set_ylim(0, y)
B.set_xticks(np.arange(k_max) + 0.5); B.set_xticklabels(range(k_max))
B.set_yticks([])
B.set_xlabel("slot p (bucket-local)")
B.set_title(f"SectionedELL: rows bucketed by degree\n"
            f"{total_sectioned} stored cells vs {real.size} flat "
            f"({100*total_sectioned/real.size:.0f}% of flat)")
B.set_aspect("equal")

fig.suptitle("ELL stores neighbour lists as a dense (n, k_max) pair; "
             "SectionedELL buckets rows by degree to dodge the padding cliff",
             y=1.02, fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The cure is one idea on top of ELL: **bucket the rows by degree**, store one small
ELL per bucket, and scatter the results back. That is
[`SectionedELL`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell_sectioned.py).
Its bucketing is `ceil(log2(degree))`: degree 1 → bucket 0 ($k_{\max}=1$), degree 2
→ bucket 1 ($k=2$), degrees 3–4 → bucket 2 ($k=4$), degrees 5–8 → bucket 3
($k=8$), and so on, so a bucket's local $k_{\max}$ is the power of two that bounds
its rows' degrees. Memory is now dominated by the *largest bucket*, not by the
worst-case row. The container holds `sections` (one `ELL` each), `row_groups` (the
original row indices per bucket, kept as host NumPy so they can serve as static
scatter indices), and the same `n_rows` / `n_cols` / `identity` scalars; a
`total_storage` property lets you check the win against a flat ELL. You build one
with
[`sectioned_ell_from_ragged`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell_sectioned.py)`(values_per_row,
indices_per_row, *, n_cols, identity=0.0, pad_index=0, bucket_by=None)` from ragged
per-row lists **(HOST-CTOR** — the per-bucket shapes are data-dependent). The right
half of @fig-operators-ell shows the same adjacency sectioned: the hub lands alone
in a wide bucket; everyone else sits in narrow ones, and the total stored cells
fall. This page gives you the *idea*; the storage economics, alignment, and tiling
live in [performance](performance.qmd#sec-storage).

### Construction, masking, and self-loops

A few more `ELL` helpers round out the storage surface; all are **HOST-CTOR or
DIFF-JAX** as noted.

- [`ell_from_dense`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell.py)`(dense,
  *, k_max=None, threshold=0.0, identity=0.0, symmetrize=False)` builds an ELL from a
  dense matrix by keeping, per row, the largest-magnitude $k_{\max}$ entries above
  `threshold` **(HOST-CTOR**, NumPy on the host — a convenience for tests and small
  workloads). One sharp edge worth knowing: top-$k$-per-row selection is **not
  symmetry-preserving** — row $i$ may keep edge $(i,j)$ while row $j$ drops $(j,i)$ —
  so the stored pattern is generally asymmetric even for a symmetric input. That
  matters for the spectral consumers whose iterative solvers assume a symmetric
  operator. Two remedies: pass `symmetrize=True` to store an exactly-symmetric kNN
  graph $\tfrac12(S + S^\top)$ over the closure $P \cup P^\top$ (but beware: a hub
  selected by every row can densify that row to the full $n$); or use the
  storage-free **matvec** symmetrisation $\tfrac12(Ax + A^\top x)$, which never
  materialises the closure — that is what `semiring_ell_rmatvec` (below) is for.
- [`ell_to_dense`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell.py)`(ell)`
  scatters an ELL back to a dense `(m, n_cols)` matrix via `scatter_add`
  **(DIFF-JAX)** — useful in tests and fallback paths. Because the scatter is
  additive, round-trip correctness holds for `REAL`; do not rely on it under a
  non-additive algebra.
- [`ell_mask`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell.py)`(ell,
  valid, *, semiring=…)` zeroes out edges that reach a masked vertex/voxel
  **(DIFF-JAX)**. This is the *incomplete-geometry* helper: the cortical **medial
  wall** is excluded from analysis, and volumetric work is restricted to a
  grey-matter mask. The subtlety the docstring is careful about: a masked edge is a
  true no-op only if its value is the algebra's **$\otimes$-annihilator** — the
  element $z$ with $z \otimes b$ equal to the additive identity for *every* $b$ —
  which is read for you from `semiring.annihilator`. For most algebras the
  annihilator coincides with the additive identity ($0$ for REAL, $-\infty$ for LOG
  and max-plus, $+\infty$ for min-plus), but `EUCLIDEAN` is the exception: its
  combine $(a-b)^2$ has *no* annihilator, so masking it by a value is impossible and
  `ell_mask(semiring=EUCLIDEAN)` raises — you must drop the columns structurally
  instead. (Prefer the `semiring=` form; the bare `identity=` form is deprecated
  precisely because it invites the identity-vs-annihilator confusion.)
- [`ell_add_self_loops`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell.py)`(ell,
  edge_attr=None, *, fill='mean', self_value=1.0)` appends an $(i,i)$ edge to every
  row, growing $k_{\max}$ by one **(DIFF-JAX)**. Self-loops are a
  graph-convolution construct, **not** part of geometric mesh adjacency
  (`mesh_k_ring_adjacency` is self-loop-free): GAT attends each vertex to itself, and
  the GCN renormalisation trick adds $\hat A = A + I$. The synthesised self-edge's
  attribute is derived from the row's existing valid edges (`'mean'`, `'add'`, or
  `'zero'`), with pad slots excluded via `values != identity`.

## The semiring abstraction {#sec-semiring}

Now the heart of the page — the idea that organises the whole unit. Look again at
@eq-operators-ellmatvec. Two operations appear: a **combine** $\otimes$ that weights
a gathered neighbour, and a **reduce** $\oplus$ that folds the weighted terms into
one number. In ordinary sparse matvec these are *fixed* — $\otimes$ is
multiplication, $\oplus$ is addition. The insight is to make them **parameters**.

A **semiring** is exactly that pair, $(\oplus, \otimes)$, equipped with an identity
for $\oplus$ (the value a padded slot must carry to vanish under the reduce).
Crucially, the *kernel* in @eq-operators-ellmatvec — the gather over the neighbour
list, the rectangular $k_{\max}$ loop — does **not depend on which pair you choose**.
Swap the algebra, keep the kernel, and the same neighbour map computes a different
geometric quantity. This is the unit's organising thesis: **change the algebra, not
the kernel.**

`nitrix` makes the algebra a first-class object. The
[`Semiring`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/_types.py)
dataclass bundles a **`monoid`** (the reduce, with `init` / `update` / `merge` /
`finalize` — the four hooks a streaming reduction needs), a **`binary_op`**
(the combine, a `Semigroup` with a single `combine` method), an **`identity`**, an
**`annihilator`** (for `ell_mask`), a `name`, and the differentiation rules
(`matmul_vjp`, `ell_matmul_vjp`). There are two flavours: the relaxed `Semiring`
makes no associativity promise (fine for the streaming kernels, which fix the
reduction order), and `StrictSemiring` additionally asserts $\otimes$ associates and
distributes — the contract a tree/cross-device reduction needs. The split is a
*signed declaration*, not a runtime check: `as_strict()` / `as_relaxed()` only
re-tag.

### The built-in algebras

`nitrix.semiring` ships six pre-built algebras
([`algebras.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/algebras.py)),
and the table is worth holding in your head, because each row is a different
operator on the *same* mesh or grid:

| Algebra | $\otimes$ (combine) | $\oplus$ (reduce) | identity | geometric meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `REAL` | $a \cdot b$ | $\sum$ | $0$ | weighted average, **diffusion / smoothing** |
| `TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS` | $a + b$ | $\max$ | $-\infty$ | morphological **dilation** / max-pool |
| `TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS` | $a + b$ | $\min$ | $+\infty$ | **erosion**, shortest path, **distance transform** |
| `LOG` | $a + b$ | $\operatorname{logsumexp}$ | $-\infty$ | soft / marginal aggregation |
| `BOOLEAN` | $a \wedge b$ | $\vee$ | `False` | reachability / connectivity |
| `EUCLIDEAN` | $(a-b)^2$ | $\sum$ then $\sqrt{\cdot}$ | $0$ | $\ell_2$ neighbour distance |

Read the first three rows as the spine of the unit. **REAL** $(+, \times)$ is the
ordinary sparse matvec: one step of diffusion, the weighted average behind mesh
smoothing and the cotangent-Laplacian apply. **max-plus** $(\max, +)$ — "combine by
adding the structuring weight, reduce by taking the max" — is morphological
**dilation**; it powers the grid morphology and max-pooling in
[volumes](volumes.qmd#sec-morphology). **min-plus** $(\min, +)$ is its mirror:
erosion, and — iterated to a fixed point — the **distance transform** and
single-source shortest path in [volumes](volumes.qmd#sec-edt). These two are the
**tropical** semirings, the algebra of dynamic programming and shortest paths.

The other three fill out the surface. **LOG** replaces the hard $\max$ with a
soft `logsumexp`, computed with an online, NaN-safe $(\text{running max},
\sum e^{\,\cdot})$ state — the same numerically-stable trick as flash-attention
softmax — for marginal / soft-min aggregations. **BOOLEAN** $(\wedge, \vee)$ is pure
connectivity: is there *any* edge carrying signal? **EUCLIDEAN** is the odd one out
and a useful teaching case: its combine $(a-b)^2$ is not associative and it applies
a non-monoidal $\sqrt{\cdot}$ projection only at the end (which is why it is a
*relaxed* `Semiring`, not strict), and it is the one algebra with **no annihilator**
— there is no $z$ for which $(z-b)^2$ is $0$ for every $b$ — so it cannot be
value-masked. You can also compose your own algebra from any `Monoid` and
`Semigroup`; the kernels do not care whether it is built-in.

@fig-operators-semiring runs one ELL operator under the three spine algebras on a
toy 1-D signal and gets three different shapes out of it — proof that the kernel is
shared and only the arithmetic changed.

```{python}
#| label: fig-operators-semiring
#| fig-cap: "One ELL operator, three semirings, one input signal. The path-graph adjacency and the gather-and-reduce loop are identical across panels; only (reduce, combine) changes. REAL (+,×) with row-normalised weights gives a weighted average (a diffusion/smoothing step). max-plus (max,+) with a flat structuring element takes the max over each window (grayscale dilation — the bump widens and the notch fills). min-plus (min,+) takes the min (erosion — the bump shrinks)."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# --- The SAME ELL operator, three algebras ----------------------------------
# A path graph on 11 vertices: each interior vertex neighbours i-1, i, i+1.
# We store it as a fixed (n, k_max=3) ELL: indices = neighbour ids, values
# = per-edge weights. Pad the two endpoints' missing slot with the algebra
# identity at matvec time (here all rows already have 3 slots via self-clamp).
n = 11
idx = np.zeros((n, 3), dtype=int)
for i in range(n):
    idx[i] = [max(i - 1, 0), i, min(i + 1, n - 1)]

# A toy per-vertex signal: a single bump plus a notch.
B = np.zeros(n)
B[3:6] = 1.0
B[7] = 0.4

def ell_matmul(values, indices, b, combine, reduce_, identity):
    # C[i] = reduce_p ( values[i,p] combine b[indices[i,p]] )
    out = np.full(n, identity, dtype=float)
    for i in range(n):
        acc = identity
        for p in range(indices.shape[1]):
            acc = reduce_(acc, combine(values[i, p], b[indices[i, p]]))
        out[i] = acc
    return out

# REAL (+, x): a row-normalised average -> one diffusion / smoothing step.
val_real = np.full((n, 3), 1.0 / 3.0)
out_real = ell_matmul(val_real, idx, B,
                      combine=lambda v, x: v * x,
                      reduce_=lambda a, b: a + b, identity=0.0)

# max-plus (max, +): a flat structuring element -> grayscale DILATION.
val_struct = np.zeros((n, 3))  # 0 = additive identity for the (+) combine
out_max = ell_matmul(val_struct, idx, B,
                     combine=lambda v, x: v + x,
                     reduce_=lambda a, b: max(a, b), identity=-np.inf)

# min-plus (min, +): same structuring element -> grayscale EROSION.
out_min = ell_matmul(val_struct, idx, B,
                     combine=lambda v, x: v + x,
                     reduce_=lambda a, b: min(a, b), identity=+np.inf)

x = np.arange(n)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 3.8), sharey=True)
for a in ax:
    a.step(x, B, where="mid", color="0.6", lw=1.5, label="input $B$")
    a.set_xlabel("vertex i"); a.set_ylim(-0.15, 1.25)
    a.grid(alpha=0.25)

ax[0].step(x, out_real, where="mid", color="C0", lw=2.4,
           label="REAL $(+,\\times)$")
ax[0].set_title("REAL: weighted average\n(diffusion / smoothing)")
ax[0].set_ylabel("value"); ax[0].legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)

ax[1].step(x, out_max, where="mid", color="C3", lw=2.4,
           label="max-plus $(\\max,+)$")
ax[1].set_title("max-plus: max over window\n(dilation)")
ax[1].legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)

ax[2].step(x, out_min, where="mid", color="C2", lw=2.4,
           label="min-plus $(\\min,+)$")
ax[2].set_title("min-plus: min over window\n(erosion)")
ax[2].legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)

fig.suptitle("One ELL operator, three semirings: swap $(\\oplus,\\otimes)$, "
             "change the geometry", y=1.03)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### The kernels that consume an algebra

Two public reductions take a `semiring=` argument and run @eq-operators-ellmatvec
(or its dense sibling). Both are **DIFF-JAX**.

- [`semiring_ell_matmul`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/ell.py)`(values,
  indices, B, *, semiring=REAL, n_cols=None, backend='auto')` is the load-bearing
  kernel of the unit — the ELL gather-and-reduce of @eq-operators-ellmatvec. It is a
  `jax.custom_vjp` whose backward routes to the algebra's `ell_matmul_vjp`; leading
  axes broadcast via `vmap`. The `backend` selects `'jax'` (the always-available
  path) or `'pallas-cuda'` (an opt-in Triton kernel that falls back to JAX, with one
  warning, when it cannot tile a shape — see
  [`ell-on-triton.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/ell-on-triton.md)).
- [`semiring_matmul`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/matmul.py)`(A,
  B, *, semiring=REAL, backend='auto')` is the *dense* analogue: $C[i,j] =
  \bigoplus_k (A[i,k] \otimes B[k,j])$ for small/dense operands. (For `REAL` on real
  inputs it equals `A @ B`; call `jnp.matmul` directly there. `nitrix`'s advantage is
  the *other* algebras.) Both have pure-JAX reference cores
  ([`_reference.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/_reference.py))
  that are the correctness floor every accelerated kernel is checked against — the
  **validation-by-reference posture** of the [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-validation)
  page: agreement with the reference within tolerance, not bit-parity across
  backends.

### The transpose direction, and differentiability

[`semiring_ell_rmatvec`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/ell.py)`(values,
indices, X, *, semiring=REAL, n_cols)` is the **adjoint** $Y = A^\top X$
**(DIFF-JAX)**. Where the matvec *gathers* — each output row pulls from its
neighbours — the rmatvec *scatters*: each row's contribution is added into the
columns it points at. It is **REAL-only**, because the additive scatter needs linear
structure: composing the two gives the **storage-free symmetric-part matvec**
$\tfrac12(Ax + A^\top x)$ that the spectral solvers use when an adjacency's top-$k$
construction (e.g. `ell_from_dense`) did not preserve symmetry. A non-additive
algebra has no meaningful "average of $A$ and $A^\top$", so any other `semiring`
raises.

On differentiability: every built-in algebra carries a hand-derived
`jax.custom_vjp`, so `jax.grad` / `jacrev` over `semiring_ell_matmul` returns
finite-difference-checked gradients with no further setup. The `indices` are integer
topology and take a zero (`float0`) cotangent — you differentiate the **values** and
the operand `B`, never the indices. The tropical algebras are subtle: the $\max$ /
$\min$ reduce is non-smooth, so their backward is the **subgradient** — a one-hot
gather routing the cotangent to the arg-max / arg-min neighbour (a single winner
takes the gradient). LOG's backward is softmax-weighted; EUCLIDEAN's uses a
normalised-difference rule with a guard. The depth of these rules — why the tropical
subgradient is the right thing, how the LOG online state differentiates — lives in
[solvers](solvers.qmd#sec-kernels); here it is enough to know the gradients are real
and checked.

### Functional edges: messages, not just stored weights

The kernels above reduce **stored** edge values. Two more public functions reduce
**computed** edge messages — the graph-neural-network pattern — and both live in
[`ell_edge.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/ell_edge.py).

- [`semiring_ell_edge_aggregate`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/ell_edge.py)`(edge_fn,
  ell, x, *, semiring=REAL, edge_attr=None)` **(DIFF-JAX)** computes, for each edge,
  a message $e[i,p] = \texttt{edge\_fn}(x_i,\, x_{\text{indices}[i,p]},\,
  \text{values}[i,p],\, (i,j)[,\, a])$ from the source feature, the neighbour
  feature, the stored scalar weight, the index pair, and an optional per-edge
  attribute vector — then reduces $\bigoplus_p e[i,p]$ under the algebra. This is the
  edge-functional mesh-conv of EdgeConv/DGCNN, GAT, MoNet, and GCN: the *reduce* half
  is identical to `semiring_ell_matmul`, only the value-of-edge step is functional
  rather than stored. (First-cut support is `REAL` / `TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS` /
  `TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS` — the algebras whose monoid is a single axis-reduction; LOG and
  EUCLIDEAN raise.) The user's `edge_fn` is responsible for letting pad slots
  contribute the identity, typically by multiplying the message by `values` (which is
  $0$ at REAL pads).
- [`ell_row_softmax`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/ell_edge.py)`(scores,
  ell)` **(DIFF-JAX)** normalises per-edge scores along the $k_{\max}$ axis so each
  row sums to 1, **masking padding** (pad slots carry `values == identity`, are
  forced to $-\infty$ before the softmax, and an all-pad row returns all-zeros rather
  than `NaN`). It is the attention pre-pass for GAT/GATv2: score the edges,
  `ell_row_softmax` them into weights, feed the weights back as the `values` of an
  edge-aggregate under `REAL`. There is also
  [`semiring_conv`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/conv.py),
  the *regular-grid* sibling of the ELL matmul — the same gather-and-reduce over a
  shift stencil rather than a neighbour list — which is the substrate the volumetric
  morphology of [volumes](volumes.qmd#sec-morphology) is built on; we meet it there.

## The apply seam {#sec-apply}

We now have two storage formats (`ELL`, `SectionedELL`) and one algebra family. The
last piece of the substrate is the seam that lets every downstream algorithm ignore
*which* storage format it was handed. That is
[`apply_operator`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/_apply.py)`(op,
x, *, semiring=None, backend='jax')` **(DIFF-JAX)**.

The contract is small and exact. `op` is a `MeshOperator` — the union type
`ELL | SectionedELL`. `x` is a per-vertex field of shape `(..., n, d)` (use a
trailing singleton `(..., n, 1)` for a scalar field). `apply_operator` dispatches on
the format — an `ELL` routes to `semiring_ell_matmul`, a `SectionedELL` to
`sectioned_semiring_ell_matmul` (which runs the per-bucket matmuls and scatters back
to original row order) — and **`vmap`s the 2-D core over the leading batch axes**.
The operator is *shared* across the batch, not itself batched. The result is the
contracted field `(..., m, d)`.

Why this matters is the line worth carrying forward: **operators vs algorithms**,
*assembly downstairs, composition upstairs*. The dependency runs one way,
`geometry → sparse`: the geometry layer *assembles* an operator (a cotangent
Laplacian, a k-ring adjacency, an icosphere pooling map) and hands it to the sparse
layer; the sparse layer *applies* it. Because `apply_operator` hides the storage
format, a flat `ELL` (the near-uniform icosphere) and a `SectionedELL` (a `recon-all`
white surface with a few high-valence vertices) **flow through identical algorithm
code** — the curvature, distortion, and geodesic-smoothing routines never branch on
storage. That is the same "factor the independent axes, don't hand-write the cross
product" discipline the eigensolver and interpolator use: the *method* (the algebra)
and the *task* (which operator) compose freely above the seam; the storage format is
dispatched once, below it.

`SectionedELL` carries its own apply pair —
[`sectioned_semiring_ell_matmul`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell_sectioned.py)
and the REAL-only adjoint
[`sectioned_semiring_ell_rmatvec`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell_sectioned.py)
— which compose to the same $\tfrac12(AX + A^\top X)$ symmetric matvec on bucketed
adjacencies. One device note that ties back to the execution classes: **building** a
`SectionedELL` is HOST-CTOR (the per-bucket shapes are data-dependent, not
`jit`-able), but **reusing** one is `jit`-friendly — the per-bucket matmuls trace
once and the dispatch is a static Python loop. So you pay the host cost **once** per
adjacency, then run the operator through `jit`-ed code many times. This is exactly
*never call a HOST op inside a jitted loop*: assemble on the host, apply on device.

## Adjacency and measures {#sec-adjacency}

Everything above needed an operator to apply. This section is where the operators
come from on the surface side — the combinatorial and metric data a mesh hands to
the substrate. The unifying line, the one that sets up the next page:

> **A mesh is a graph** — the faces tell you *who neighbours whom* — **plus a
> metric** — the vertex positions give you *the weights*.

The graph half is pure topology (face indices); the metric half is geometry (vertex
coordinates). @fig-operators-adjacency shows both halves on a level-1 icosphere: the
1-ring degree (combinatorics) and the per-vertex area (metric). Hold this split: the
[cotangent Laplacian](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent) on the next page is exactly
*the graph adjacency, reweighted by a metric quantity* (the cotangent edge weights),
and the [mass matrix](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-mass) is the vertex-area diagonal.

```{python}
#| label: fig-operators-adjacency
#| fig-cap: "A mesh is a graph plus a metric, on a level-1 icosphere (42 vertices). Left: the combinatorics — each vertex coloured by its 1-ring degree (valence). Twelve vertices inherit valence 5 from the icosahedron's corners; the rest are valence 6. This is the graph the adjacency operator encodes. Right: the metric — each vertex coloured by its barycentric area A_i, the per-vertex measure the area-weighted operators use. The graph says who-neighbours-whom; the metric says how-much-each-weighs."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# --- Build a level-1 icosphere by hand (icosahedron + one subdivision) -------
phi = (1.0 + np.sqrt(5.0)) / 2.0
V = np.array([
    [-1, phi, 0], [1, phi, 0], [-1, -phi, 0], [1, -phi, 0],
    [0, -1, phi], [0, 1, phi], [0, -1, -phi], [0, 1, -phi],
    [phi, 0, -1], [phi, 0, 1], [-phi, 0, -1], [-phi, 0, 1],
], dtype=float)
V /= np.linalg.norm(V, axis=1, keepdims=True)
F = np.array([
    [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
    [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
    [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
    [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
], dtype=int)

def subdivide(V, F):
    cache = {}
    V = V.tolist()
    def mid(i, j):
        key = (min(i, j), max(i, j))
        if key in cache:
            return cache[key]
        m = (np.array(V[i]) + np.array(V[j])) / 2.0
        m /= np.linalg.norm(m)
        V.append(m.tolist()); cache[key] = len(V) - 1
        return cache[key]
    NF = []
    for a, b, c in F:
        ab, bc, ca = mid(a, b), mid(b, c), mid(c, a)
        NF += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
    return np.array(V), np.array(NF)

V, F = subdivide(V, F)   # level-1 icosphere: 42 vertices

n = len(V)

# --- The COMBINATORICS: 1-ring degree (who-neighbours-whom) ------------------
adj = [set() for _ in range(n)]
for a, b, c in F:
    for i, j in [(a, b), (b, c), (c, a)]:
        adj[i].add(j); adj[j].add(i)
degree = np.array([len(s) for s in adj])

# --- The METRIC: per-vertex (barycentric) area -------------------------------
v0, v1, v2 = V[F[:, 0]], V[F[:, 1]], V[F[:, 2]]
face_area = 0.5 * np.linalg.norm(np.cross(v1 - v0, v2 - v0), axis=1)
vert_area = np.zeros(n)
for k in range(3):
    np.add.at(vert_area, F[:, k], face_area / 3.0)

# Orthographic projection onto the xy-plane for plotting; colour by quantity.
X, Y = V[:, 0], V[:, 1]
order = np.argsort(V[:, 2])  # painter's order, back to front

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5.2))

# Draw edges once.
edges = set()
for a, b, c in F:
    for i, j in [(a, b), (b, c), (c, a)]:
        edges.add((min(i, j), max(i, j)))

for A, vals, title, cmap, lbl in [
    (ax[0], degree, "combinatorics: 1-ring degree\n(the GRAPH: who neighbours whom)",
     "viridis", "valence (5 or 6)"),
    (ax[1], vert_area, "metric: per-vertex area $A_i$\n(the WEIGHTS the operators use)",
     "magma", "vertex area"),
]:
    for i, j in edges:
        A.plot([X[i], X[j]], [Y[i], Y[j]], color="0.8", lw=0.5, zorder=1)
    sc = A.scatter(X[order], Y[order], c=vals[order], cmap=cmap,
                   s=90, edgecolor="k", lw=0.4, zorder=2)
    A.set_title(title); A.set_aspect("equal"); A.set_xticks([]); A.set_yticks([])
    fig.colorbar(sc, ax=A, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04, label=lbl)

n5 = int((degree == 5).sum())
fig.suptitle(f"A mesh is a GRAPH plus a METRIC. Level-1 icosphere: {n} vertices, "
             f"{n5} have valence 5 (the icosahedron's corners), the rest valence 6",
             y=1.0, fontsize=10.5)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### The Mesh container, and how a mesh is born

A
[`Mesh`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py) is
the minimal pair the canonical notation names: `vertices` $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n
\times 3}$ and `faces` $F \in \mathbb{Z}^{m \times 3}$ (each face a triple of vertex
indices). It is a JAX pytree — *both* arrays are children, so a `Mesh` flows through
`jit` / `grad` as an operand; surface optimisers grad through `vertices` while
`faces` (integer topology) takes a zero cotangent.

The analytic mesh this whole unit validates against is the **icosphere**, built by
[`icosphere`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)`(n_iterations=0)`
**(HOST-CTOR)**. It starts from the 12-vertex icosahedron and recursively
subdivides: each triangle splits into four, each new edge-midpoint is projected back
onto the unit sphere. After $n$ iterations it has $10 \cdot 4^n + 2$ vertices
($n=1 \to 42$, $n=2 \to 162$, …, $n=7 \to 163842$, matching FreeSurfer's `ico7`).
The icosphere is the unit's **convergence-under-refinement testbed**: it is a
sequence of ever-finer discrete approximations of the *same* smooth object (the unit
sphere), so you can compute a quantity at each level and watch the error shrink
toward the analytic truth (a sphere has mean curvature $H = 1/r$ everywhere; its
total Gaussian curvature is $4\pi$ by Gauss–Bonnet). Its valence is near-uniform —
twelve valence-5 vertices, the rest valence-6 — which is exactly why its adjacency
stays a flat `ELL` and never sections.

For multiscale work there is the
[`IcosphereHierarchy`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)
(a tuple of meshes at levels $0..L$ plus parent–child tables), built by
[`icosphere_hierarchy`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)`(max_level)`
or, from your own meshes,
[`icosphere_hierarchy_from_levels`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py).
Unlike `Mesh` / `ELL`, the hierarchy is **deliberately not a pytree** — its parent
tables are host-side construction metadata; it is a HOST-CTOR artefact you use once
on the host to emit array-bearing operators, which then cross the `jit` boundary. Two
constructors turn it into cross-level ELLs:
[`icosphere_cross_level_adjacency`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)`(hierarchy,
coarse_level, fine_level)` (a coarse→fine pooling adjacency with a $1/0$ validity
indicator in its `values`) and
[`icosphere_bary_upsampler`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)`(hierarchy,
coarse_level, fine_level)` (a fine-from-coarse barycentric upsampler); both are
**HOST-CTOR** and feed the spherical pooling/unpooling discussed in
[graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd#sec-pooling).

### The combinatorial operator: k-ring adjacency

The graph half of the mesh becomes an applyable operator through
[`mesh_k_ring_adjacency`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)`(mesh,
*, k=1, binary=True, include_self=False, format='ell')` **(HOST-CTOR)**. The
construction climbs in the obvious way: read the undirected edges off the faces to
get the **1-ring** (each vertex's immediate neighbours), then BFS-expand $k$ steps to
the **k-ring** (neighbours within $k$ hops). The flags set what the operator *means*:

- `binary=True` stores a $1$ at each neighbour and $0$ (the ELL identity) at pads —
  a plain adjacency, the right thing to reduce under a tropical algebra (count,
  dilate, reach).
- `binary=False` stores $1/|\mathcal{N}(i)|$ per row — a **row-stochastic**
  random-walk operator, so a `REAL` matvec is a neighbour *average*: one step of
  diffusion / smoothing.
- `include_self` adds the vertex to its own neighbour list (off by default — geometric
  adjacency is strictly the neighbours; the centre weight is the Laplacian's job).
- `format` is `'ell'` (flat), `'sectioned'`, or `'auto'` — and `'auto'` is the
  policy in action: it sections **only** when the max/median degree ratio exceeds
  $\sim 2$. Near-uniform template surfaces (icosphere, `fsaverage`) stay flat;
  irregular-valence surfaces (e.g. marching-cubes output with a few high-valence
  vertices) section. The return type is therefore `ELL | SectionedELL` — which is
  exactly why `apply_operator` exists to absorb the difference.

### The metric operators: areas and normals

The metric half is three differentiable, pure-JAX measures — all **DIFF-JAX**, all
grad-through-`vertices`, the hot-path ingredients of the area-weighted operators on
later pages.

- [`face_areas`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)`(mesh)`
  returns $\tfrac12 \lVert (v_1 - v_0) \times (v_2 - v_0) \rVert$ per face — half the
  magnitude of the face cross product. The intuition is the standard one from linear
  algebra: the cross product of two edge vectors has magnitude equal to the area of
  the parallelogram they span, so half of it is the triangle.
- [`vertex_areas`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)`(mesh,
  *, scheme='voronoi')` is the **lumped mass diagonal** $A_i$ — the per-vertex area
  that weights every area-aware operation ($M^{-1}L$ mean curvature, areal
  distortion, the geodesic-smoothing solve $(M + tL)x = Mx_0$). It splits each
  triangle's area among its three vertices, two ways. `'barycentric'` gives each
  vertex $\text{area}/3$ — cheap, always non-negative, the safe fallback.
  `'voronoi'` (default) is the mixed-Voronoi rule of Meyer et al. (2003): for a
  non-obtuse triangle vertex $i$ gets its circumcentric-dual area $\tfrac18(\cot
  \angle_k \lvert x_i - x_j \rvert^2 + \cot \angle_j \lvert x_i - x_k \rvert^2)$, but
  for an **obtuse** triangle — where the Voronoi cell would fall outside the triangle
  — the obtuse vertex gets $\text{area}/2$ and the others $\text{area}/4$. Both
  schemes satisfy a useful invariant exactly: $\sum_i A_i = \sum_f \text{area}_f$
  (the per-vertex areas partition the total surface area). The obtuse branch is
  **load-bearing on real cortical surfaces** and never exercised by the
  icosphere — a reminder that the analytic oracle, while perfect for convergence
  checks, does not stress every code path.
- [`compute_vertex_normals`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)`(vertices,
  faces)` gives unit per-vertex normals $n_i$. Each face contributes its
  un-normalised cross-product normal $(v_1 - v_0) \times (v_2 - v_0)$ — whose
  magnitude is twice the face area, so **larger faces weight more** — scattered onto
  its three vertices; the per-vertex sum is then L2-normalised (a zero-area vertex
  maps to the zero vector, not `NaN`). The orientation $n_i$ is what later turns an
  unsigned curvature magnitude into a *signed* mean curvature.

That is the full surface-adjacency surface: a `Mesh`, an analytic icosphere (and its
hierarchy) to validate against, one combinatorial operator (`mesh_k_ring_adjacency`),
and three metric measures (`face_areas`, `vertex_areas`, `compute_vertex_normals`).
Graph + metric. The [next page](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent) fuses them into
the cotangent Laplacian.

## Further reading

- J. Kepner, J. Gilbert (2011). *Graph Algorithms in the Language of Linear
  Algebra*. SIAM. — the semiring / GraphBLAS view that "change the algebra, not the
  kernel" formalises; the canonical text on matrix-shaped graph computation.
- D. Maclagan, B. Sturmfels (2015). *Introduction to Tropical Geometry*. AMS Graduate
  Studies in Mathematics 161. — the min-plus / max-plus (tropical) semirings, the
  algebra behind erosion, dilation, and the distance transform.
- N. Bell, M. Garland (2009). "Implementing Sparse Matrix-Vector Multiplication on
  Throughput-Oriented Processors." *Proc. SC '09*. — the comparative study of sparse
  formats (including ELL/ELLPACK and the hybrid HYB) on GPUs that motivates ELL's
  fixed-shape bet.
- F. Baccelli, G. Cohen, G. J. Olsder, J.-P. Quadrat (1992). *Synchronization and
  Linearity: An Algebra for Discrete Event Systems*. Wiley. — the foundational
  treatment of max-plus algebra.
- M. Meyer, M. Desbrun, P. Schröder, A. H. Barr (2003). "Discrete
  Differential-Geometry Operators for Triangulated 2-Manifolds." In *Visualization
  and Mathematics III*, Springer. — the mixed-Voronoi vertex-area rule used by
  `vertex_areas`.
- P. Veličković, G. Cucurull, A. Casanova, A. Romero, P. Liò, Y. Bengio (2018).
  "Graph Attention Networks." *ICLR*. — the attention aggregation that
  `ell_row_softmax` + `semiring_ell_edge_aggregate` implement.

## See also

- ELL storage and primitives: [`sparse/ell.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell.py)
- Sectioned (degree-bucketed) ELL: [`sparse/ell_sectioned.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell_sectioned.py)
- The format-agnostic apply seam: [`sparse/_apply.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/_apply.py)
- The algebras and protocols: [`semiring/algebras.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/algebras.py),
  [`semiring/_types.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/_types.py)
- The reductions: [`semiring/ell.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/ell.py),
  [`semiring/matmul.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/matmul.py),
  [`semiring/ell_edge.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/ell_edge.py),
  the reference cores [`semiring/_reference.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/_reference.py)
- The mesh container, icosphere, adjacency, and measures: [`sparse/mesh.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)
- Design records: [`docs/design/sparse-specialisations.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/sparse-specialisations.md),
  [`docs/design/ell-on-triton.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/ell-on-triton.md)
- Neighbouring pages: [foundations](foundations.qmd) (execution classes, validation) ·
  [Laplace–Beltrami](laplace-beltrami.qmd) (the cotangent stiffness and mass) ·
  [volumes](volumes.qmd) (the semiring machine on grids) ·
  [performance](performance.qmd#sec-storage) (ELL/SectionedELL storage economics) ·
  [solvers](solvers.qmd#sec-kernels) (the differentiable-kernel depth) ·
  [graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd) (pooling and the graph mirror) ·
  sibling unit: [the statistical modelling suite](../statistics/index.qmd).
